planting.note: Do not attempt to transplant from the wild — collection is illegal in most states and wild-dug plants virtually never survive; sourcing from a specialist native orchid nursery with documented propagation lineage is the only viable path.

directions: This record exists primarily for identification and documentation purposes: Large Whorled Pogonia is one of the rarest native orchids in eastern North America and is essentially impossible to cultivate intentionally — it depends on a precise, poorly understood relationship with specific soil fungi that cannot be reliably recreated, and plants removed from the wild almost invariably die within one to two seasons. Never collect or transplant from wild populations, as this is illegal under state protection laws across most of its range and causes direct population harm. If encountered naturally on a property, the best stewardship is to protect the surrounding soil and woodland canopy, avoid foot traffic near colonies, and note the location for reporting to your state natural heritage program. A single stem bearing a whorl of five to six leaves topped by a solitary maroon-green flower in May–June is the signature form; plants may remain dormant underground for multiple years before re-emerging.

planting.note: Cultivation is essentially impossible — this plant has no chlorophyll and cannot photosynthesize, surviving entirely by parasitizing soil fungi; transplanting from the wild is both destructive and futile, and no reliable nursery propagation method currently exists.

directions: This record exists for identification and educational purposes only: Crested Coralroot is a mycoheterotrophic orchid with no green tissue whatsoever — it produces no chlorophyll and cannot photosynthesize, obtaining all of its carbon and nutrients by parasitizing underground mycorrhizal fungi. Because of this complete dependence on a specific fungal network that is itself tightly bound to the root systems of surrounding trees, the plant cannot survive transplanting and cannot be cultivated in any conventional sense. Do not collect or disturb wild plants under any circumstances; populations are naturally sparse and fragile, and removal kills the plant with certainty. If you find it growing on your property in dry deciduous woodland or cedar glades of the southeastern US, treat the surrounding area as a preserve: avoid soil disturbance, refrain from applying herbicides or fertilizers, and maintain the canopy trees that support the fungal network the plant depends on. The striking purple-brown striped flowers appear on a leafless stalk in June–July and are the only visible sign of a plant that may spend most years entirely underground.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown stock in spring after last frost; provide a fence, trellis, or shrub edge for the twining stems to climb, and site in well-drained soil with some afternoon shade in hotter climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade, providing a sturdy trellis, fence, or shrub for support; Grape Honeysuckle is a non-invasive native alternative to the notorious Japanese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica) that offers nearly all the same wildlife value — tubular yellow flowers for hummingbirds and long-tongued bees in June–July, followed by clusters of red berries consumed by thrushes, waxwings, and other songbirds in fall — without any of the aggressive, smothering growth habit that makes Japanese honeysuckle a serious ecological threat. One of its most distinctive features is its perfoliate leaves, where paired leaves fuse at the base so the stem appears to pass directly through a single disk-shaped leaf, giving the foliage a sculptural, unusual appearance. It is substantially more cold-hardy than most ornamental honeysuckle vines, thriving in zones 3–8, and is well-suited to the upper Midwest and Great Plains where few ornamental vines are fully reliable. Established vines are drought-tolerant once rooted.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early to midsummer, or plant rooted container stock in spring; site at forest margins or on structures with dappled light, as this species naturally inhabits the woodland edge rather than open, exposed positions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, loamy to rocky soil in part shade to full sun along a trellis, fence, or forest edge; Limber Honeysuckle is a native twining vine found across northern and central North America from Quebec to British Columbia and south into the Ozarks and Appalachians, scrambling up shrubs and small trees along forest margins where it is an important nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds during their spring and early-summer residency. The flowers range from orange-yellow to red and appear in May–June in clustered whorls at the stem tips, followed by translucent red to orange berries in late summer that are eaten by thrushes, vireos, and other woodland songbirds. Like Lonicera prolifera, it has perfoliate leaves near the stem tips where the uppermost pair of leaves fuse into a disk, though in this species the leaves beneath the flower clusters are typically distinctly bluish-green. It is non-invasive and well-behaved in the garden, rarely exceeding 15 feet, making it one of the best native vine choices for small to medium trellises in northern gardens.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or press stem cuttings into waterlogged soil or gravel at the edge of a cold, clear, slow-moving stream or pond; plants root readily from stem nodes in contact with wet substrate and quickly colonize suitable aquatic margins.

directions: Grow in cold, clean, slow-moving to still water or in consistently saturated soil at the water's edge — ideal conditions are a spring-fed stream with a gravel substrate and constant cool temperatures below 70°F; One-rowed Watercress is closely related to the common watercress (Nasturtium officinale) sold in grocery stores and is harvested and eaten in the same way, but its flavor is noticeably more peppery and pungent, making it preferred by those who want a stronger bite in salads, sandwiches, and garnishes. It can be distinguished from common watercress by its smaller, rounder leaflets and, botanically, by its seeds being arranged in a single row inside the seed pod rather than the double row of N. officinale. Like common watercress, it is best harvested in cool weather — spring and fall — as summer heat triggers bolting and intensifies bitterness. Only harvest from water sources confirmed to be free of agricultural runoff, animal grazing upstream, and waterborne pathogens such as Fasciola hepatica (liver fluke), which can colonize watercress in contaminated streams; thoroughly cook the greens if water quality is uncertain.
